file_name: Ramdhana and Pratiwi - 2023 - Perbandingan kinerja model convolutional neural network pada klasifikasi kanker kulit.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used deep learning, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN), to classify skin cancer. It compared the performance of InceptionV3 and EfficientNetB0 models on the HAM10000 dataset for skin cancer detection and classification.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work comparing the performance of two specific Convolutional Neural Network models (InceptionV3 and EfficientNetB0) on the HAM10000 dataset for skin cancer classification. It includes a dedicated 'METODE' (Methods) section detailing data preprocessing, model architecture, and training procedures, as well as a 'HASIL DAN PEMBAHASAN' (Results and Discussion) section reporting specific accuracy metrics (0.7681 for InceptionV3 and 0.9809 for EfficientNetB0). The study aims to design and evaluate a classification system, producing new empirical results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Perbandingan Kinerja Model Convolutional Neural Network pada Klasifikasi Kanker Kulit
brief_summary: This study aimed to design a skin cancer classification system using deep learning to improve early detection and diagnostic accuracy. The researchers compared the performance of two Convolutional Neural Network models, InceptionV3 and EfficientNetB0, on the HAM10000 dataset. To address data imbalance, the dataset was preprocessed by balancing classes and rescaling images to 228x228 pixels. The evaluation results showed that EfficientNetB0 achieved a significantly higher accuracy of 0.9809 compared to InceptionV3's 0.7681. Consequently, the study concludes that EfficientNetB0 is the superior model for skin cancer detection among the two tested architectures.

lesion_types_studied.details: Mentioned in abstract and introduction as a type of skin cancer included in the HAM10000 dataset. The study balanced classes to include this type, though specific counts for melanoma alone are not isolated in the final balanced subset description (which states 327 per class).

all_task_types.target: Discrete skin cancer diagnostic categories (e.g., melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, nevi, benign keratosis-like lesions, actinic keratoses, vascular lesions, dermatofibroma).
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly aims to design a 'skin cancer classification system' using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs). The models (InceptionV3 and EfficientNetB0) predict discrete classes from the HAM10000 dataset. The study balanced the dataset to 7 classes (though Table 3 mentions 5 classes for the specific training/validation split, the abstract and introduction list multiple specific cancer types). Performance is evaluated using accuracy metrics (0.7681 for InceptionV3 and 0.9809 for EfficientNetB0), which are standard for classification tasks, not regression.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (The-HAM10000 Dataverse / HAM-10000 Mnist Skin Cancer)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from Harvard University containing dermoscopic images of skin lesions. The original dataset contained 10,015 images with metadata including lesion_id, image_id, dx, dx_type, age, sex, localization, and cell_type. The study utilized this dataset, performed preprocessing to handle class imbalance, and rescaled images to 228x228 pixels.

original_total.others: Not explicitly listed individually but total 10,015

use.training: Used for training InceptionV3 and EfficientNetB0 models after balancing classes to 327 samples per class and rescaling to 228x228 pixels.
use.validation: Used for validation during the training process (1145 samples total, derived from the balanced subset).
use.testing: Null (The paper reports accuracy on the training/validation split or implies the final evaluation was based on the trained model's performance metrics, but does not explicitly define a separate held-out test set with specific counts distinct from the validation split in the final reporting table).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. The comparison between InceptionV3 and EfficientNetB0 was based on descriptive accuracy metrics (0.7681 vs 0.9809) and inference times, without statistical significance testing.

HAM10000.notes: The study balanced the dataset to 327 samples per class (5 classes total) and rescaled images to 228x228 pixels. EfficientNetB0 also demonstrated faster inference time (5.3 minutes) compared to InceptionV3 (7.8 minutes).

architecture_used.pretraining: Transfer learning utilized; pre-trained models were used (implied by 'utilisasi pada module keras dari tensorflow' and standard practice for these models, though specific pre-training dataset like ImageNet is not explicitly detailed in the text, it is referenced via Tan & Le, 2019 for EfficientNetB0).

InceptionV3.description: Consists of a series of convolution layers with 1x1, 5x5, and 3x3 filters, and Maxpooling layers.

EfficientNetB0.description: Selected for minimum parameter count and promising results.

training_details.data_augmentation: Used as part of data preprocessing (referencing Gulakala et al., 2022)
training_details.input_normalization: Rescaling to 228x228x3, pixel values normalized to range 0-1 (divided by 255)

classification_or_output_head.note: Text mentions 5 classes in Table 3 but lists 6 types in the dataset description. The balanced subset used 327 samples per class.

architecture_used.approximate_parameters: Not explicitly reported (EfficientNetB0 noted for having 'minimum parameter count')

preprocessing_techniques: Class balancing (resampling to 327 samples per class to address class imbalance)
Pixel value normalization (rescaling pixel values to range 0-1 by dividing by 255)
Data augmentation (applied as part of preprocessing to improve model performance)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied as part of data preprocessing to improve model performance and generalization, referencing Gulakala et al. (2022). Specific geometric or color transform parameters (e.g., rotation, flipping, zoom ranges) were not explicitly reported in the text.

conclusions: EfficientNetB0 demonstrated superior performance for skin cancer classification compared to InceptionV3, achieving an accuracy of 0.9809 versus 0.7681.
EfficientNetB0 exhibited greater computational efficiency, requiring a shorter inference time (5.3 minutes) compared to InceptionV3 (7.8 minutes).
The study utilized the HAM10000 dataset, addressing class imbalance by resampling to 327 samples per class for five specific skin lesion types.
The findings suggest that EfficientNetB0 is a more suitable model for future medical applications due to its higher accuracy and faster inference speed.
The research highlights the importance of using representative datasets with balanced classes to improve the generalization and diagnostic accuracy of CNN-based skin cancer detection systems.
important_remarks: The study artificially balanced the HAM10000 dataset by discarding excess samples to create a subset of only 327 images per class, resulting in a total of 1,635 training images. This severe data reduction limits the model's ability to learn from the full diversity of the original dataset and may lead to overfitting or poor generalization to real-world imbalanced distributions.

The dataset preprocessing excluded classes with fewer than 327 samples (specifically vascular lesions with 142 and dermatofibroma with 115), meaning the model was not trained or evaluated on these skin cancer types, limiting its clinical applicability for comprehensive skin cancer detection.

The paper does not report a separate, held-out test set; evaluation metrics (accuracy) appear to be derived from the training/validation split or internal validation, raising concerns about data leakage and over-optimistic performance estimates.

No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) was performed to validate the difference in accuracy between InceptionV3 (0.7681) and EfficientNetB0 (0.9809), making it unclear if the performance gap is statistically robust.
Critical hyperparameters such as the optimizer, loss function, learning rate, and batch size are not explicitly reported, hindering reproducibility.
The study relies solely on accuracy as a metric, which is insufficient for imbalanced medical classification tasks; metrics like sensitivity, specificity, precision, recall, and AUC are missing.

The dataset used is the HAM10000, which is known to have demographic biases (e.g., overrepresentation of fair-skinned individuals from specific geographic regions), and the study does not discuss the impact of this bias on the model's external validity.
